stories.title: PGA Championship Begins Tomorrow at Aronimink — Full Preview

x_posts.label: Post 1
x_posts.text:
Tomorrow: the 108th PGA Championship begins at Aronimink Golf Club.

The last time the PGA was here? 1962. Gary Player won.

Scheffler is defending. Spieth needs it for the Slam. Rahm needs a bounce-back. Should be a week. #PGAChamp

facebook.long_form:
One of the most compelling weeks in recent major championship history begins tomorrow, and we are here for it.

The 108th PGA Championship tees off Thursday at Aronimink Golf Club in Newtown Square, Pennsylvania — a course that hasn't hosted a PGA Championship since Gary Player claimed the Wanamaker Trophy back in 1962. It's a precision golf course, one that rewards iron accuracy and demands delicate putting on enormous, undulating greens. That description could not suit the current favorites more perfectly.

Scottie Scheffler is the defending champion and the overwhelming favorite at the top of the betting board, but he enters on a streak of three consecutive runner-up finishes that would be remarkable at any talent level. Rory McIlroy seeks another major to add to his Grand Slam collection. Cameron Young — No. 3 in the world and hunting his first major — has been in career-best form. Jon Rahm, Xander Schauffele, and a field of 156 players complete a week that is loaded with storylines.

And then there's Jordan Spieth. The 32-year-old needs only the PGA Championship to complete the career Grand Slam. He said this week he "feels closer than ever." Maybe this is finally the week.

Round 1 coverage begins on ESPN Thursday. Who are you watching?
facebook.image_caption: The 108th PGA Championship. Tomorrow. Aronimink Golf Club. 64 years since this course last hosted golf's second major. Let's go. ⛳ #PGAChamp

article.headline: PGA Championship Preview: Aronimink Hosts Its First Major in 64 Years — Here's What to Watch

stories.title: Scheffler Seeks Back-to-Back — Can He End His Runner-Up Streak?

x_posts.label: Post 2-1
x_posts.text:
Scottie Scheffler's last three tournaments: runner-up, runner-up, runner-up.

He's the defending PGA Champion. He's the No. 1 player in the world.

Aronimink suits him perfectly. Three in a row can't last forever. #PGAChamp

x_posts.label: Post 2-2
x_posts.text:
Only Brooks Koepka (2018-19) and Tiger Woods have won back-to-back PGA Championships in stroke play.

Scheffler has a chance to join that list this week at Aronimink. #PGAChamp

facebook.long_form:
Scottie Scheffler is playing like the best golfer in the world. The scorecard just keeps lying about it.

Three consecutive events, three consecutive runner-up finishes. He finished second at the Masters when Rory McIlroy held on. He lost a playoff at the RBC Heritage to Matt Fitzpatrick. And then second again in the most recent event. No player has finished in that many consecutive solo second-place results since Sergio Garcia pulled off the same curious feat back in 2014.

Now he arrives at Aronimink as the defending PGA Champion, the world's top-ranked player, and a man who is clearly overdue for a victory. The course suits his game exceptionally well. Aronimink's large greens and tight fairways demand exactly the kind of elite iron play that has made Scheffler the finest ball-striker of his generation.

Winning back-to-back PGA Championships in stroke play would put Scheffler in the company of Brooks Koepka (2018-19) and Tiger Woods. There is an argument to be made that no active player is better equipped, statistically, for this specific course.

Do you think the runner-up streak ends this week?
facebook.image_caption: Runner-up. Runner-up. Runner-up. Now Scheffler defends his PGA title at Aronimink, a course that was built for his game. Something has to give. ⛳ #PGAChamp #Scheffler

article.headline: Scheffler Has Been Runner-Up Three Times in a Row. Now He's Defending His PGA Title.

stories.title: Spieth's Grand Slam Quest — Aronimink Is His Next Best Chance

x_posts.label: Post 3
x_posts.text:
Jordan Spieth has won the Masters. The US Open. The Claret Jug.

He needs only the PGA Championship.

Aronimink is his latest chance to complete the career Grand Slam. He said he "feels closer than ever." 🏆 #PGAChamp

facebook.long_form:
The moment Jordan Spieth won The Open Championship at Royal Birkdale in 2017, he joined one of golf's most exclusive clubs — a player who had won three of the four major championships. He was 24 years old, with a long career ahead and what felt like an inevitable fourth title just around the corner.

Nine years later, he's still waiting for the PGA Championship.

Spieth arrives at Aronimink having won the 2015 Masters, the 2015 US Open, and the 2017 Open Championship. The PGA is the only one missing from his collection, and completing the career Grand Slam would make him just the seventh player in the modern era to hold all four major championship titles. He would join Jack Nicklaus, Tiger Woods, Ben Hogan, Gary Player, Gene Sarazen, and Rory McIlroy in that group.

The 32-year-old has tried before, more than once, with his best result being a tie for third at the 2019 PGA Championship. But he has said this week that he "feels closer than ever," and Aronimink — a course that rewards precision iron play rather than raw power — may be more suited to his game than the bombers' courses that have sometimes hurt his chances.

History. Redemption. A story nine years in the making. It starts tomorrow.
facebook.image_caption:
Masters ✅ US Open ✅ The Open Championship ✅ PGA Championship ❓

Spieth needs one more. Aronimink is his latest chance. #PGAChamp #JordanSpieth

article.headline: Can Jordan Spieth Finally Complete the Career Grand Slam at the PGA Championship?

stories.title: Nelly Korda 4 Points From LPGA Hall of Fame — Queen City Starts Tomorrow

x_posts.label: Post 4
x_posts.text:
Nelly Korda has 23 of 27 required LPGA Hall of Fame points.

She's 4 away from induction.

The Kroger Queen City Championship starts TOMORROW in Cincinnati. A win gets her one step closer. 🏌️‍♀️ #LPGA

facebook.long_form:
While the men tee it up at Aronimink tomorrow, the LPGA Tour has its own compelling storyline unfolding just a few hours away.

The Kroger Queen City Championship begins Thursday at Maketewah Country Club in Cincinnati — and Nelly Korda arrives four points short of LPGA Hall of Fame induction.

Korda currently sits at 23 of the 27 points required for Hall of Fame eligibility. She has already won three times in 2026 — the Tournament of Champions, the Chevron Championship, and the Riviera Maya LPGA Classic — and each additional win brings her one point closer to one of golf's most honored milestones. A win in Cincinnati this week would push her to 24 of 27.

Jeeno Thitikul, who won the Mizuho Americas Open just last week, is also in the field and enters as one of the most dangerous players on the tour in current form.

For Korda, who at 27 is already one of the most decorated players of her generation, the Hall of Fame conversation isn't a distant prospect — it's a few more trophies away. Cincinnati could be the next step.

Will Korda win the Queen City and inch closer to history?
facebook.image_caption: Nelly Korda: 23/27 LPGA Hall of Fame points. 4 away. The Queen City Championship starts tomorrow in Cincinnati. ⛳🏆 #LPGA #NellyKorda

article.headline: Nelly Korda Is 4 Points From the LPGA Hall of Fame — Her Next Chance Starts Tomorrow

stories.title: LIV Golf's Eleven at Aronimink — Rahm and DeChambeau Have Something to Prove

x_posts.label: Post 5
x_posts.text:
11 LIV Golf players tee it up at Aronimink this week.

Rahm. DeChambeau. DJ. Cam Smith. Hatton. Niemann.

Both Rahm and DeChambeau had rough Masters. The PGA Championship is redemption time. #PGAChamp #LIVGolf

facebook.long_form:
Eleven LIV Golf players are in the field at Aronimink this week — and they arrive with something to prove.

Jon Rahm, the leader of the LIV Golf season standings, barely made the cut at the Masters before finishing deep in the field. Bryson DeChambeau, cleared from a wrist injury and showing renewed form, missed the cut at Augusta entirely. For both players, the PGA Championship represents an opportunity to show that their move to LIV hasn't taken them out of the major championship conversation.

The full LIV contingent includes Rahm, DeChambeau, Dustin Johnson, Cameron Smith, Tyrrell Hatton, Joaquin Niemann, Tom McKibbin, Thomas Detry, Martin Kaymer, David Puig, and Elvis Smylie. They qualified through various pathways including world ranking and past champion exemptions.

Rahm is listed at +1500 in the betting market. DeChambeau has drifted further out. But major championships have always been a great equalizer — and if either of LIV's biggest stars can contend at Aronimink, it would send a message to those who have written off the tour's players as no longer capable of competing at golf's highest level.

Which LIV player do you think has the best chance at Aronimink?
facebook.image_caption: 11 LIV Golf players enter Aronimink. Rahm leads their season standings. DeChambeau is healthy again. Both had rough Masters. Redemption time. ⛳ #PGAChamp #LIVGolf

article.headline: LIV Golf's Aronimink Contingent: Rahm and DeChambeau Are Hungry for a Major Moment
